sentences.text: Yes.
Yeah.
So there was no way of joining while they were marching.
I didn't belong.
I had no papers, nothing.
So we walked to this railroad station.
And in Poland, you had to purchase the ticket first in order to go through the gate to the, the thing.
So we came to the railroad station.
I am very emotional, because I still remember the, the anxiety, still remember the fear.
Here I was out without the armband, without the name, with nothing.
A Jewish girl in the midst of all of these--
In the midst, in the daytime, 2 o'clock in the afternoon.
And then we come to the station.
And there was safe.
So they bought me the ticket to Krakow, right, to get the--
This Mrs.
Klimashevska.
And they-- so I went through the gate to the, to the platform where the subway comes.
It's a platform, something else?
Yes.
Platform.
And I see the Ukrainian black uniformed police, and the Polish police, German police.
And this transport, the people-- like so two by two-- they were going into this train.
It was a-- on this side was a special designated train.
And here, what am I going to do?
I had nothing.
I don't belong.
I mean, that's it.
That always-- this is it.
Now they will take me away, shoot me.
This is it.
And then the-- I notice the Polish policeman there knew know me, Mr.
Pach I think was-- P-A-C-H-- a Polish policeman.
He knew my father.
My father left was still in the ghetto and my stepmother.
So he said to the Ukrainian, the, the-- they were the German helpers.
They were this SS.
She is from the transport, too.
So I was-- yes, he helped me.
I was on the side.
So this Ukrainian pushed me into-- he thought that maybe I want to run away.
Because these people-- that I want to run away.
So he pushed me into the train.
And there, I was in the train.
I sit down next to-- he came to sit down next to me.
And here, the fear-- I had no armband.
I had this.
And they were-- this was a special train.
They were up there.
And there were the peasants, you know.
They were sitting there.
It was dark.
They were taking out their bread to eat, their kielbasa, whatever.
And I-- there I-- what am I going to do?
What am I going to do?
So he came to me.
And he took out a little notebook.
And he said, write a message to your father.
You know, he was sure I was afraid.
I thought that anybody that looks at me will recognize me.
But I had the kerchief.
And I had this blonde hair.
And I wrote down, I said, there is a girl that with with the name-- some.
I don't remember what her name was suppose to be.
She wants to run away.
So she will-- you will get her papers for the, the-- this was for to go to Germany to work.
I lacked these proper-- the words.
So we were going.
It was a long time.
We arrived in this, in Krakow, right at night.
And I didn't see this girl.
I didn't see the papers, nothing.
I was just lost in space-- no name, no-- with the fear, with this terrible, this fear.
So they-- it was late.
They march us again.
You know, it was this, this head of this-- you know, the leader of this transport.
And the police, they march us, you know, with the streets in Krakow at night.
We came to a big old high school.
You know, they looked like, let's say, Cambridge or something.
And every high school had the-- it was like a square, you know, with buildings, and the square inside, you know, for the playground, like in the prisons have now.
So they, they brought us in.
They say to roll call, stand like two rows.
And there, I saw the German police-- you know, they had different uniforms-- with dogs, and Polish police surrounding this.
And somehow, he was-- this leader-- was in front.
And he took out the pile of papers.
And he started reading off names.
Whoever is called, step aside, to just double check nobody run away or what, and double check.
And here I was.
I said, what I am going to do?
That's it.
That's finished.
See the dogs.
And then he read the paper.
A few people were, were-- stayed.
They were not on the list or something.
Said, go back.
And I remembered a name that nobody responded to.
So the second time around, go back, he started reading the names.
People started to step out.
Then he came to this name called Sypek, Julia.
I didn't know if she didn't hear some Sypek, Julia.
I said in Polish, here I am.
And I stepped out.
And he gave me her transport papers.
And that's how I became Sypek, Julia.
Yes.
And I looked at the paper.
This was not an ID, which was the, the, the main thing, to have this real, the ID, you know, they can [INAUDIBLE] like this.
No, she was-- at the time, she was born in-- she must have been 31 years old, right?
I was 16.
So what to do?
I got the duplicate, right?
And there-- so immediately, I made the-- a two out of the one, 11, 21.
You know, so that was already close to something.
And then I went into this thing on this, you know, wooden-- how you call these things-- the double, you know, like children have but with the just the straw.
We were there.
And I was there a few days with this somehow.
Another transport came in.
I was looking-- because we were there a few days.
You had to go through the doctors.
You had to go through [NON-ENGLISH].
What is that?
Delousing.
Delousing.
You had to other some-- you know, they were asking your background and, you know, to know that to send you, but send you to work.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Can I mention something now which is important, that this feeling remained with me.
It was so strong, this identity.
Without any preparations, I started-- you know, I had to know the-- that I work late around here.
And I worked as an interpreter, a Polish interpreter-- I have to interject this now-- and with my very good Polish, right?
And I remember somewhere, I had to wait in court.
You know, the case came, the-- as an interpreter when I was called, sometimes I was sitting next to some relatives.
I never said that I was Jewish.
And I immediately identified.
I went back to my other identity.
I was this Polish.
How that strong was.
The one of the women that was waiting with her relatives, and she said, you know, all these lawyers are [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, they're Jews.
All these lawyers are Jews.
And, and I didn't react.
I said, who cares?
I don't know.
You know, I am doing my-- was getting paid, right, $25 an hour for waiting.
Nothing.
Yeah.
But I didn't have to do.
I could have told her off.
But that was-- I just said it, because it was so strong.
I started with this identity.
And after a few days came another transport-- again, this, this fear of that and this self-preservation there is.
You cannot describe it.
I see another transport come.
And the head of this transport, I see my former girlfriend.
She had good looks.
You know what I mean-- blue eyes.
And she was with somebody else.
It-- I never even wanted to see her to say hello to her.
I just-- it was an instinct, an instinct maybe somebody knows that she's Jewish.
Nobody was telling me these things.
You just knew how to protect yourself.
But later, when we went to the, to the, the-- you know-- and delousing, right?
We had to take a-- went through the doctors, rights?
First, first going there, looking for lice.
And I had bleached hair, which was unusual for a young girl, as I'm a peasant girl, right?
So this was already a bad thing.
I-- we left the clothes and then came back and walking naked around with the Polish police.
And we had to pick up this-- I showed you this ID-- the number, right?
You had to have this number tag and pick up your clothes.
I take my clothes.
I turn around.
And I see this Sonja.
And when she saw me, I thought she's going to faint.
And she made such a ah!
And my instinct was I turned around.
And I walked out.
And how these things happened probably like when you touch fire or something, right?
It was instinct.
I didn't want to have anything to do with her because she was Jewish.
Now I have to, I guess, make it shorter, right?
Well, tell us then--
Now the main thing-- this was already this.
I have this identity.
But I had no, no [INAUDIBLE] card.
The [INAUDIBLE] card, that was this important ID.
This was in 1942.
And the Russians, you know, they started to invade, go to Russia-- the Germans.
Yes.
Krakow and my town were on the direct line to the roads leading and the railroads to Lwow and to Russia, right?
This was going from west to east and north to Warsaw.
And so they had to rebuild the, you know, the, the bad roads and build new bridges, the new railroads and things.
I guess that was my luck.
So when I was finally-- after a few days-- assigned to work, I was not sent to Germany.
I was sent back to Poland, straight to Poland, a few kilometers from Krakow to Prokocim, right next to this where it was filmed.
Right near Plaszow.
Right near Plaszow.
These were like suburbs, one next to, to each other.
And then this Prokocim were camps, the Jewish camps for the Jewish workers.
It was like, you know, one next to the other, like, you know, Budapest, you know, one.
And so I was sent there.
And here, again, this dilemma-- and I had no ID.
So they put us in barracks again.
There was-- I got lice from-- there were a lot of this Ukrainians and dirty.
Ah, whatever, you know-- there was no food.
There was no something.
It was hopeless.
They were sending us to work.
I was going without any ID.
I was walking.
And I always had a kerchief on my head.
I was walking to Plaszow to eat from the-- this was not a concentration camp yet.
IT was a working camp, labor camp.
I was getting food.
There was a Jewish canteen.
They had, you know, giving out soup.
And I was going with my whatever thing to the Jewish thing.
And they looked.
And they-- anybody could have recognized me.
I was walking around without my armband, without my papers and milling among--
Nobody identified me.
I know that they later knew about who I was, but--
So I was a few days in this situation.
And in this lager, going to work, in this Plaszow or-- I think it was-- came Christmas.
I was there Christmas time.
These were the Poles and Russians.
1942, So they gave us-- the Germans gave us big little dinner, whatever.
And there were also the people, the [NON-ENGLISH].
Did you hear about [NON-ENGLISH], the Russians that became, you know, German soldiers.
They were this.
Yeah.
OK.
Then I didn't know what to do again.
There was one girl that came that was in this-- lived in this something.
And I talked to her.
And she told me I got a job.
And the German word is [GERMAN].
The, the barracks, there were the, the headquarters of the construction, yeah?
I got a job as a cleaning woman.
I said, really?
Where is it?
And where is something?
And I went to this.
I-- because we could walk out, right?
We were not, we were not-- the Jews were working.
This was a Viennese construction firm, road and, and railroad construction bridges.
And they were working there near Krakow.
They belonged to this [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, the railroad things, the one that supervised the trains, all these trains from Krakow, right?
It was in the Shoah.
So I really worked for the, the, the railroads, for the German railroad.
So I went.
She showed me.
I went to such a barrack.
This was the [GERMAN].
And he-- this was the construction supervisor.
That's right?
He had his office.
And there was a, a Jewish bookkeeper.
Because there was another camp.
This was still a labor camp.
So they were taking some people.
I came.
And I-- and you know, he said maybe there is, I think, work.
Yeah, I was hired.
I was hired as a cleaning woman, girl.
And I was supposed to get up at-- in the morning and come and start the fire and the, and the iron thing, little stove.
And I was given a little room opposite this, this canteen.
Because they could take whatever they wanted.
They paid, let's say, in coal, right, the Polish owners.
There was a little room, tiny room, with four beds.
There were some-- already was this Wanda-- she lived there-- and two Russian girls that somehow were stranded in Poland.
They were in this transport.
So I was going there.
And let's say in the morning, I came.
I didn't know how to start the fire.
He was coming, this Austrian-- was an Austrian helping me.
I was there for two weeks.
I had hardly anything to eat.
And I am sure this is how it worked.
And--
The, the, they was coming, the representative, some former lawyer, Jewish lawyer, well-dressed, from the camp, right?
He was coming in the morning.
And he was getting-- spoke German.
He was getting assignment from this [GERMAN], you know, the-- my boss.
Two weeks-- I was two weeks there.
Yes, OK, go ahead.
OK.
Then after these two weeks--
Yes.
They liked me.
I spoke German.
I was young, whatever, doing my cleaning.
I know that the Jews that worked there recognized me, right?
So he saw that I had nothing to eat.
They had these supervisors.

And the workers, the Austrians and the German workers, right, that worked for this SS foreman or whatever they-- it's mostly foreman and mechanics, right, crane operators-- that Jews were coming every day to work the menial, and Polish people, too.
They had their own canteen for rations.
They were coming.
They ate three-- they were coming for breakfast, for lunch, and dinner, had the same food.
After two weeks, he said, I see you have nothing to eat.
I will-- you come with me.
He brought me to this canteen.
And there were Jewish girls working there as help.
So he said-- he came, he spoke to this chef, a woman that lived there, a Austria.
And she said, Frau [INAUDIBLE], this is your new help.
Yes.
Until I was there.
So now I will make it shorter.
Yes.
I was in this situation.
I was working.
I got now a better room in the building with another girl from this transport.
And we were working in this living there and working as kitchen helps, starting the fire, starting--
A whole year.
I was there since, I know, January 8th till February 2nd of '40--
Till February the 2nd, 1944.
Yes, yes, yes.
I had that good food.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
I know that time is short.
But I have to describe this.
I-- the Jews were coming and going.
And I work with the Polish people.
I had no ID.
Anybody could look at me.
I lived in fear day and night, second by second, somebody will open the door.
Somebody will come in.
Somebody will expose me.
I didn't have the papers yet.
Not yet-- I only had to this for this, this transport, you know, name.
They didn't ask for my ID so far.
And the Jews were still coming and working.
They had a place where they had the coal.
And the-- a Jewish man was working in this coal, you know?
And I had to identify myself.
And if he could, then he helped me.
I was getting-- Do you know his name?
No.
I know he was from [INAUDIBLE] from something.
And I forgot his name, very nice--
I-- one day, you know, already in July, I, I was always in.
I had this somehow that not to expose myself, not to walk as if I can help it.
And they were telling me, it was hot.
Why don't you go out?
And this [INAUDIBLE], you know my roommate, she had no idea.
She said, let's go for a walk.
Let's go [INAUDIBLE].
I just couldn't help it, always this kerchief, because my roots came back.
And they were asking me.
I had something on my head, always in a kerchief because my roots.
And I had no way of, of bleaching or no way of nothing.
Disappeared.
So I-- what's happened, right?
And then with the black hair, you look more Jewish.
So we walked to this railroad station, walking in there, you know, because this is where they were building this.
And there was, was already sabotage or whatever.
There was a Ukrainian with the black uniform guarding this, right?
So he came over.
He started-- he saw this-- my roommate was tall and blonde.
Saw me, start talking.
And then he looks at me.
And he said, you are Jewish.
She's Jewish.
So I, I am just talking about this, this self-preservation.
So I started laughing. you know, just it came out instinctively.
I don't know-- maybe intelligence.
Maybe fear.
I started-- maybe fear.
But I-- this was a constant thing.
I had to know what to say and how to, to, you know, when I was peeling potatoes and they started talking about church in the next room, I was going in and changing the conversation.
And my former boss said, you see.
Look, [NON-ENGLISH], you know-- you are the youngest and the smallest.
And you talk all the time, so to prevent the others to, to question me or to talk.
Anyhow, I started to laugh.
He said, why do you laugh?
I said, well, you paid me a compliment.
The Jewish girls are pretty.
So this stunned me.
I don't know how I, I came to, you know-- Jewish girls are pretty.
It just came out.
So this [INAUDIBLE] said, she's not Jewish.
She lives with me.
She's working.
So that was really one--
The closest call.
Yes.
So then--
I got, I got the false papers, right, through some Polish people were coming.
I was getting vodka rations.
So I was selling it on the black market, cigarettes.
And that's how--
So a Polish underground was working.
So they were able to come even to visit.
And I bought the Polish papers.
Somebody.
Yeah, yes, yes, yes.
They knew all but my-- you know, whatever I was telling them, where I was born.
That was changed after I give them a picture.
So I had an ID.
And this [INAUDIBLE].
But I didn't say anything about it.
Because it didn't correspond exactly with what it was.
And through my stay there-- first of all the fear of being recognized, always alert, always listening to people who is coming and who is going, or who is something, knowing what to say.
There were instances that I thought that's it.
They required ration, food rations, for the workers, too, right?
In order to get these workers, you had to be, to be registered, right, and the [NON-ENGLISH], where the salt mines were.
I wasn't there.
So the following day, said, you have to bring your ration something.
You have to be rationed, too.
Because we ate in this canteen without the rations.
So I-- that's it.
This is every week, every few days, this is the end.
This is the end.
This is 1943.
So I, I-- at night, I was praying to my-- this beloved grandmother-- not God, no [INAUDIBLE], help me.
Help me.
The following day came the pay master, right?
And he came in.
And he brought-- we were like three or something, four.
And he brought these four rations.
And he gave it to the, the chef, and mine including.
How, why he did it and how it happened, maybe he knew.
I don't know.
It was a miracle.
Because I couldn't go to ask for this.
Then it started, you know-- I've heard what was going on.
And the lagers, they started shooting.
I spoke German.
Came a, an SS, you know, Miller.
I know his name was Miller.
He was like this from the like this get from another camp.
And every time a German-- you know, they would came representative from the railroad, you know, from Krakow, I was sent to wait on them.
The dining room was upstairs.
The same thing-- the Polish girls didn't want, you go, right?
So I brought him his tray, this SS, right, look straight into his eyes.
And he said in German, danke, Fraulein-- you know, thank you, miss.
Another one, some wanted to give me a tip.
And so this fear, you know, was accumulating, accumulating, right?
And then I wanted how to escape.
Because I felt this is already-- the fire is already under my feet.
And the Americans landed already in Italy.
How did you know that?
Because we had a radio.
We had the German-- you know, they had the radio.
The other girls, too, we were cleaning upstairs, the dining rooms, the something.
I heard all this news, the BBC.
You know, we just did.
So I knew about the Americans.
There were papers, too, right.
But they didn't say anything.
I didn't know anything what was going in Africa or nothing.
So I thought that they will see-- there were many instances that were just on the brink of being discovered.
But let's, let's return to that.
To that.
Yeah.
That had not only a major effect.
I already planned how to further escape.
I cannot stay here anymore.
Because it's getting bad.
Now the, the Austrians and Germans, they had the right to ask for help, slave work, right?
And they were allowed, because all these slave workers that went to Germany went to work in the fields or factories, I mean, whatever, right?
And there was one man that I knew him being from the Alp, from Tyrol.
And I remember at night with the candle, I had an atlas.
And I knew--
Yeah.
Yes.
OK.
